segments.title: Hermes sisters and the treaty carrier tradeoff
segments.summary: The Patreon live stream frames a Royal Navy Hermes-sisters counterfactual around Washington Treaty tonnage limits, cruiser-carrier compromises, and forward presence needs.
segments.body: The discussion captures the scenario's core setup. Dr. Clarke explains why carrier cumulative tonnage mattered more than the single-ship limit, why sub-10,000-ton ships were attractive under the treaty system, and why Hermes is best treated as a small cruiser-carrier and forward-deployable presence asset rather than a fleet carrier.

evidence.note: The opening frames the modified patron prompt and explains how Washington Treaty carrier tonnage pushes navies from ideal 27,000-ton designs toward smaller carrier counts and tradeoffs.
The treaty discussion emphasizes that there is no cumulative limit on sub-10,000-ton ships, linking the loophole to Britain's cruiser and sea-lane-control priorities.
The Hermes section gives the ship's origins, specifications, cruiser-carrier characterization, and usefulness as a carrier presence asset around the empire.
